discussion_comments.comment_body:
Should we use this mechanism with a default/built-in task id generator that is implemented with uuid to maintain backwards compatibility?

This way we won’t get None for `task_id_generator` and avoid the `else: task_id = uuid()`

Not critical, but can make it more elegant, WDYT?

Yaa that will make it more cleaner...

with this `task_id_generator` will either be custom generator or `uuid` as the default and never `None`... right? will push...

'task_always_eager has no effect on send_task',
             ), stacklevel=2)
 
+        # If the caller did not supply a task_type (i.e. a plain
+        # send_task("name", ...) call), look it up in the local registry
+        # and apply its execution options as defaults.  We intentionally
+        # skip this when task_type was already provided (e.g. from
+        # Task.apply_async) because apply_async already merged exec
+        # options — doing it again would override explicit caller values.
+        #
+        # Use the underlying registry directly here so send_task() does not
+        # auto-finalize the app (or raise when autofinalize=False) merely to
+        # check whether a locally registered task exists. Remote/unregistered
+        # task names should still be sendable without finalizing the app.
+        resolved_from_registry = False
+        if task_type is None:
+            registry = self._tasks
+            get = getattr(registry, 'get', None)
+            if callable(get):
+                task_type = get(name)
+            else:
+                try:
+                    task_type = registry[name]
+                except KeyError:
+                    task_type = None
+            resolved_from_registry = task_type is not None
+        if resolved_from_registry and hasattr(task_type, '_get_exec_options'):
+            get_exec_options = task_type._get_exec_options
+            if inspect.ismethod(get_exec_options):
+                task_exec_options = get_exec_options()
+            else:
+                task_exec_options = None
+            if task_exec_options:
+                # Only merge non-None values so we don't override
+                # defaults with None.
+                filtered_opts = {k: v for k, v in task_exec_options.items()
+                                 if v is not None}
+                if filtered_opts:
+                    options = dict(filtered_opts, **options)
+
+        # Some execution options (time_limit, soft_time_limit, expires)
+        # are also passed as explicit arguments to create_task_message.
+        # Pop them from options to avoid "got multiple values for argument"
+        # errors; use the task-level value as fallback when the caller
+        # did not provide an explicit value.
+        if time_limit is None:
+            time_limit = options.pop('time_limit', None)
+        else:
+            options.pop('time_limit', None)
+        if soft_time_limit is None:
+            soft_time_limit = options.pop('soft_time_limit', None)
+        else:
+            options.pop('soft_time_limit', None)
+        if expires is None:
+            expires = options.pop('expires', None)
+        else:
+            options.pop('expires', None)

that's a valid concern. With None as the default, app.send_task('registered', time_limit=None) was indistinguishable from omitting the argument. 
I've addressed this in the latest commit.

discussion_comments.comment_body: Agreed this is a latent issue — `flush()` already discards the exception via `_`, so there's no reason to retain it. I left the cleanup out of this PR on purpose to keep the change scoped to the reproduction in #10273; the    tuple-shape change ripples into `flush()` unpacking and `extend_buffer()` semantics, which deserves its own focused review. Happy to open a follow-up PR once this merges — or fold it in here if you'd prefer a single change, just let me know.

return getattr(request, 'shadow', None) or default
 
 
+def get_actual_ignore_result(task, req):
+    """Return the effective ignore_result, with request overriding task.
+
+    If req provides an explicit ignore_result, that value is used;

discussion_comments.commented_code:
@@ -154,6 +154,25 @@ def get_task_name(request, default):
     return getattr(request, 'shadow', None) or default
 
 
+def get_actual_ignore_result(task, req):
+    """Return the effective ignore_result, with request overriding task.
+
+    If req provides an explicit ignore_result, that value is used;
+    otherwise task.ignore_result is returned.
+    """
+    if req is None:
+        return task.ignore_result
+
+    actual = getattr(req, 'ignore_result', None)
discussion_comments.comment_body:
`Context.py` has defined class-level `ignore_result`([ref](https://github.com/celery/celery/blob/main/celery/app/task.py#L92)). So `getattr(task_request, 'ignore_result', None)` returns False (the class default) even when `ignore_result` was never set by the request. Without the `__dict__` check, it's impossible to distinguish "explicitly set to False" from "not set at all, class default returned". The check correctly sets `actual = None` to trigger the task-level fallback.

This is non-obvious and essential. Without a comment, a future reader will likely think the branch is dead code and remove it, re-introducing the bug.
```python
def get_actual_ignore_result(task, req):
    ...
    actual = getattr(req, 'ignore_result', None)

    # Context defines `ignore_result = False` at class level (see Context
    # in celery/app/task.py). getattr() above would return the class default
    # (False) even when the request never set it explicitly, making it
    # impossible to distinguish "override=False" from "not set". We check
    # __dict__ to detect only instance-level (i.e., explicitly set) values.
    if isinstance(req, Context) and 'ignore_result' not in req.__dict__:
        actual = None
```

discussion_comments.commented_code:
@@ -154,6 +154,25 @@ def get_task_name(request, default):
     return getattr(request, 'shadow', None) or default
 
 
+def get_actual_ignore_result(task, req):
+    """Return the effective ignore_result, with request overriding task.
+
+    If req provides an explicit ignore_result, that value is used;
+    otherwise task.ignore_result is returned.
+    """
+    if req is None:
+        return task.ignore_result
+
+    actual = getattr(req, 'ignore_result', None)

+import inspect
+
 from vine.utils import wraps
 
 from celery.exceptions import Ignore, Retry
 from celery.utils.time import get_exponential_backoff_interval
 
 
+def check_reserved_arg_availability(fun):
+    arg_spec = inspect.getfullargspec(fun)
+    if 'autoretry_opts' in set(arg_spec.args):
+        raise ValueError(

discussion_comments.comment_body:
`if autoretry_opts is None`, it's just to not set `{}` as default argument because it's mutable. 

Even if it would be safe to do in this case, it's better to avoid it.
